does: This paper develops Concept Bottleneck Sparse Autoencoders (CB-SAE), a post-hoc framework that enhances sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability of large vision-language models. The approach introduces two new metrics—interpretability and steerability—to systematically evaluate SAE neurons and identify which contribute meaningfully to both understanding and controlling model behavior. The authors then prune low-utility neurons and augment the SAE latent space with a lightweight concept bottleneck module aligned to user-defined concepts. Evaluated across LVLMs and image generation tasks, CB-SAE achieves substantial improvements: 32.1% gain in interpretability and 14.5% in steerability, directly addressing the practical gap between discovering features and being able to understand and manipulate them.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of sparse autoencoders for language model interpretability, extending recent efforts to apply SAEs beyond language models to large vision-language models. It responds to a critical limitation in prior SAE research: while sparse autoencoders have shown promise for discovering interpretable features and enabling steering, most discovered neurons lack practical utility for downstream tasks. The paper combines ideas from mechanistic interpretability, concept-based explanation methods, and the bottleneck architectures used in concept-based interpretability work. It also draws on earlier steerability research that seeks to modify model behavior through latent intervention, while introducing new evaluation methodology to systematically quantify both interpretability and steerability rather than treating them separately.
enables: This work opens several future directions for controllable and interpretable vision-language models. By demonstrating that user-defined concept sets can be reliably injected into learned representations, it enables practical applications where practitioners need models to reason in terms of human-understandable concepts. The framework could facilitate safer model deployment by allowing runtime concept-based steering and auditing. The interpretability and steerability metrics introduced here could become standard tools for evaluating other feature discovery methods beyond SAEs. More broadly, CB-SAE suggests a general template for retrofitting arbitrary neural network components with human-aligned concept spaces, potentially enabling fine-grained control over large multimodal models without expensive retraining, and supporting mechanistic understanding of how vision and language processing intertwine in learned representations.
